expanded_description: A comprehensive examination of blood composition—the specialized fluid connective tissue that circulates through the cardiovascular system—detailing the components of whole blood: plasma (water, proteins, electrolytes, nutrients, wastes) and formed elements (erythrocytes, leukocytes, platelets). Emphasis is placed on the structure, function, production (hematopoiesis), and regulation of each component, including erythrocyte function (oxygen transport, hemoglobin), leukocyte classification (granulocytes, agranulocytes) and immune functions, and platelet function (hemostasis, thrombosis). Clinical correlations include anemia, polycythemia, leukocytosis, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and hematologic malignancies.

core_concept.principle: Blood is a specialized fluid connective tissue composed of plasma (55% of volume) and formed elements (45%): erythrocytes (red blood cells) transport oxygen and carbon dioxide; leukocytes (white blood cells) mediate immune defense; platelets (thrombocytes) initiate hemostasis—all produced through hematopoiesis in the bone marrow, with each lineage regulated by specific growth factors and cytokines.
core_concept.explanation:
Blood is the primary transport medium of the body, delivering oxygen and nutrients to tissues, removing waste products, mediating immune responses, and maintaining hemostasis. It is composed of cellular components (formed elements) suspended in an aqueous protein-containing solution (plasma).

Plasma (55% of blood volume): 92% water, 7% proteins (albumin—colloid osmotic pressure; globulins—antibodies, transport; fibrinogen—coagulation), 1% electrolytes, nutrients, wastes, hormones. Serum is plasma without clotting factors.

Erythrocytes (Red Blood Cells, RBCs): 4.2-6.2 million/μL. Biconcave discs (increases surface area, allows deformation). No nucleus or organelles. Primary function: oxygen transport via hemoglobin (Hb). Hemoglobin: tetramer of globin (2α, 2β chains) and heme (iron-containing porphyrin). O₂ binding cooperative; CO₂ transported as bicarbonate. Lifespan 120 days; removed by spleen. Production: erythropoiesis (bone marrow) regulated by erythropoietin (EPO, kidney).

Leukocytes (White Blood Cells, WBCs): 4,500-11,000/μL. Nucleated cells with immune functions. Classified as:
Granulocytes: Neutrophils (50-70%)—phagocytes, first responders to bacterial infection; Eosinophils (1-4%)—anti-parasitic, allergic responses; Basophils (<1%)—allergic responses, histamine release.
Agranulocytes: Lymphocytes (20-40%)—B cells (antibody), T cells (cellular immunity), NK cells; Monocytes (2-8%)—migrate to tissues as macrophages, phagocytosis, antigen presentation.

Platelets (Thrombocytes): 150,000-450,000/μL. Small anucleate cell fragments derived from megakaryocytes. Function: hemostasis—adhere to damaged endothelium, aggregate, activate coagulation cascade, form platelet plug. Lifespan 7-10 days. Production: thrombopoiesis regulated by thrombopoietin (TPO, liver).

Hematopoiesis: Blood cells derived from hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) in bone marrow. Lineages: myeloid (erythrocytes, platelets, granulocytes, monocytes) and lymphoid (lymphocytes). Regulated by growth factors: erythropoietin (EPO), granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF), thrombopoietin (TPO), interleukins.

Understanding blood composition is essential for clinical practice: anemia (reduced RBCs/Hb), polycythemia (increased RBCs), leukocytosis (increased WBCs—infection, leukemia), leukopenia (decreased WBCs—chemotherapy, bone marrow failure), thrombocytopenia (low platelets—bleeding risk), thrombocytosis (high platelets—thrombosis risk), and hematologic malignancies (leukemia, lymphoma, myeloma).

image.alt: Centrifuged blood sample: plasma (top, 55%), buffy coat (leukocytes and platelets, <1%), erythrocytes (bottom, 45%). Microscopic images of erythrocytes (biconcave discs), leukocytes (neutrophil, eosinophil, basophil, lymphocyte, monocyte), platelets (small fragments).
image.caption: Blood Composition: Plasma (55%, water, proteins, electrolytes), buffy coat (leukocytes, platelets, <1%), erythrocytes (45%). Formed elements include erythrocytes (RBCs), leukocytes (WBCs, five types), and platelets.

sections.section_title: Introduction: Blood as a Specialized Fluid Connective Tissue
sections.introduction:
Blood is the body's primary transport and communication medium, circulating through the cardiovascular system to deliver oxygen and nutrients, remove waste products, coordinate immune defense, and maintain hemostasis. Unlike other connective tissues, blood is fluid, with cells suspended in an aqueous matrix (plasma).

Blood volume in adults is approximately 5 liters (8% of body weight). Composition: plasma (55%) and formed elements (45%). The formed elements include erythrocytes (red blood cells, RBCs), leukocytes (white blood cells, WBCs), and platelets (thrombocytes). Each component has specialized functions and is produced through hematopoiesis in the bone marrow.

The study of blood composition—hematology—is fundamental to clinical medicine. Complete blood count (CBC) with differential is one of the most commonly ordered tests, providing information about oxygen-carrying capacity, immune status, and hemostatic potential. Abnormalities in blood components indicate a wide range of disorders: anemia (fatigue, pallor), infection (leukocytosis, left shift), bleeding disorders (thrombocytopenia), and hematologic malignancies (leukemia, lymphoma).

This section covers the structure, function, production, and regulation of plasma, erythrocytes, leukocytes, and platelets, integrating basic science with clinical applications.

introduction_image.alt: Centrifuged blood tube showing layers: plasma (clear yellow), buffy coat (white layer of WBCs/platelets), erythrocytes (red). Components of plasma: water, albumin, globulins, fibrinogen, electrolytes, nutrients, wastes.
introduction_image.caption: Blood Components: Centrifugation separates whole blood into plasma (55%), buffy coat (<1%), and erythrocytes (45%). Plasma contains water, proteins, electrolytes, and solutes.

key_concept.definition: Blood consists of plasma (water, proteins, electrolytes, solutes) and formed elements: erythrocytes (oxygen transport), leukocytes (immune defense), and platelets (hemostasis)—each lineage derived from hematopoietic stem cells in bone marrow.
key_concept.context:
This composition enables blood to perform multiple essential functions: (1) Transport: O₂, CO₂, nutrients, hormones, wastes; (2) Regulation: pH buffering, temperature, fluid balance; (3) Protection: immune defense (leukocytes, antibodies), hemostasis (platelets, coagulation factors).

Clinical relevance: Complete blood count (CBC) quantifies formed elements; abnormalities indicate disease. Anemia (low RBCs) causes fatigue, pallor, dyspnea; polycythemia (high RBCs) increases viscosity, thrombosis risk. Leukocytosis (high WBCs) suggests infection or malignancy; leukopenia increases infection risk. Thrombocytopenia (low platelets) causes bleeding; thrombocytosis increases thrombosis risk.

image.alt: Diagram showing functions: transport (O₂, CO₂, nutrients, wastes), regulation (pH, temperature, fluid balance), protection (immune, hemostasis).
image.caption: Blood Functions: Transport, regulation, and protection—each enabled by specific plasma and cellular components.

sections.key_concept: Blood components are produced through hematopoiesis; each lineage has specialized structure-function relationships; abnormalities cause characteristic clinical syndromes.

classifications.value: Best for understanding the composition and functions of the acellular component of blood.
characteristics: Water (92%): Solvent for solutes; maintains blood volume; enables heat transfer.

Proteins (7%): Albumin (3.5-5.0 g/dL)—60% of plasma proteins; maintains colloid osmotic pressure (oncotic pressure); transports fatty acids, bilirubin, drugs. Globulins (2.0-3.5 g/dL)—α, β, γ globulins; antibodies (immunoglobulins) produced by plasma cells; transport proteins (transferrin, haptoglobin, lipoproteins). Fibrinogen (200-400 mg/dL)—soluble precursor of fibrin; essential for coagulation; acute phase reactant.
Electrolytes: Na⁺ (135-145 mEq/L), K⁺ (3.5-5.0 mEq/L), Cl⁻ (98-108 mEq/L), HCO₃⁻ (22-28 mEq/L), Ca²⁺ (8.5-10.2 mg/dL), Mg²⁺, PO₄³⁻.
Nutrients: Glucose, amino acids, lipids, vitamins.
Wastes: Urea, creatinine, bilirubin, uric acid.
Hormones: Endocrine hormones transported to target tissues.
classifications.behavior:
Plasma is the liquid medium that suspends formed elements and transports solutes. Its composition reflects overall body homeostasis: plasma glucose reflects nutritional state; electrolytes reflect fluid balance; creatinine reflects renal function; albumin reflects nutritional and liver status; coagulation factors reflect hemostatic potential.

Serum is plasma without clotting factors (fibrinogen removed during coagulation). Serum is used for most chemistry tests (electrolytes, enzymes, hormones) because clotting does not interfere.

Clinical correlations: Hypoalbuminemia (low albumin) occurs in liver failure, nephrotic syndrome, malnutrition → reduced oncotic pressure → edema. Hyperproteinemia (high protein) occurs in multiple myeloma (monoclonal gammopathy), dehydration. Coagulopathies (low fibrinogen, factor deficiencies) cause bleeding; elevated fibrinogen (acute phase response) increases thrombosis risk.

examples.process: Serum Protein Electrophoresis (SPEP) in Multiple Myeloma
examples.explanation:
Serum protein electrophoresis separates plasma proteins by charge: (1) Normal pattern: albumin peak (60%), followed by α1, α2, β, γ globulins (polyclonal). (2) Multiple myeloma: malignant plasma cells produce monoclonal immunoglobulin (M-protein) → sharp spike (M-spike) in γ region (or β region depending on isotype). (3) Quantification: M-spike level correlates with tumor burden; used for diagnosis and monitoring. (4) Complications: Hyperviscosity (high M-protein), renal failure (light chains), immunosuppression (normal immunoglobulins suppressed). (5) Treatment: Chemotherapy, proteasome inhibitors, immunomodulatory drugs, stem cell transplantation.

Significance: SPEP illustrates how plasma protein composition reflects underlying pathology, and how monoclonal gammopathies (multiple myeloma, Waldenström's macroglobulinemia) are diagnosed and monitored.

image.alt: Normal SPEP: albumin peak, α1, α2, β, γ globulins (polyclonal). Multiple myeloma: sharp monoclonal spike (M-spike) in γ region. Immunofixation identifies isotype (IgG kappa, etc.).
image.caption: Serum Protein Electrophoresis (SPEP): Normal pattern shows polyclonal immunoglobulins. Multiple myeloma produces a monoclonal spike (M-spike), representing malignant plasma cell proliferation.

classifications.value: Best for understanding red blood cell structure, hemoglobin function, oxygen transport, and disorders of RBCs (anemia, polycythemia).
characteristics: Structure: Biconcave disc (7-8 μm diameter) → maximizes surface area-to-volume ratio (for gas exchange) and enables deformation through capillaries (spleen, narrow vessels). No nucleus, no organelles; lacks mitochondria (ATP via glycolysis). Cytoplasm packed with hemoglobin (95% of dry weight).

Hemoglobin (Hb): Tetramer of globin chains (2α, 2β in adults—HbA) and heme (iron-containing porphyrin). Each heme binds one O₂ molecule cooperatively (sigmoidal O₂-Hb dissociation curve). HbA₁c: glycated hemoglobin; reflects average glucose over 2-3 months; used in diabetes monitoring.

Oxygen Transport: O₂ binds Hb in lungs (high PO₂); O₂ released in tissues (low PO₂). Factors shifting curve right (decreased affinity, increased O₂ release): ↑CO₂, ↓pH (Bohr effect), ↑temperature, 2,3-DPG. Factors shifting curve left (increased affinity, decreased O₂ release): ↓CO₂, ↑pH, ↓temperature.
CO₂ Transport: 70% as bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻) via carbonic anhydrase; 20% bound to Hb (carbaminohemoglobin); 10% dissolved.

Production (Erythropoiesis): Occurs in bone marrow (adults). Requires iron, vitamin B12, folate, erythropoietin (EPO). EPO produced by kidney (peritubular interstitial cells) in response to hypoxia (↓O₂, anemia). Lifespan: 120 days. Destruction: aged RBCs removed by spleen (extravascular hemolysis).
classifications.behavior:
Erythrocytes are specialized for oxygen transport. Their biconcave shape provides high surface area and flexibility; lack of mitochondria prevents O₂ consumption (so they don't compete with tissues for O₂). Hemoglobin binds O₂ cooperatively, enabling efficient loading in lungs and unloading in tissues.

Erythropoiesis is tightly regulated by EPO. Hypoxia (anemia, high altitude, lung disease) increases EPO production → stimulates RBC production → increases O₂-carrying capacity. Iron, B12, and folate are essential for hemoglobin synthesis and DNA synthesis (RBC proliferation).

Clinical correlations: Anemia—reduced RBC mass or Hb. Causes: blood loss (hemorrhage), hemolysis (destruction), decreased production (iron deficiency, B12/folate deficiency, bone marrow failure, chronic kidney disease—low EPO). Symptoms: fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, tachycardia. Polycythemia—increased RBC mass. Primary: polycythemia vera (myeloproliferative neoplasm). Secondary: chronic hypoxia (COPD, high altitude), EPO-secreting tumors (renal cell carcinoma). Increased viscosity → thrombosis risk. Sickle cell disease—HbS mutation (β6 Glu→Val) causes hemoglobin polymerization under hypoxia → RBC sickling → hemolysis, vaso-occlusion. Thalassemia—imbalanced globin chain synthesis (α or β) → ineffective erythropoiesis, hemolysis, microcytosis.

examples.process: Iron Deficiency Anemia: Pathophysiology and Diagnosis
examples.explanation:
Iron deficiency anemia is the most common anemia worldwide: (1) Pathophysiology: Insufficient iron for hemoglobin synthesis → microcytic (small RBCs), hypochromic (pale) RBCs. Causes: blood loss (GI, menorrhagia), inadequate intake, malabsorption. (2) Laboratory: Low hemoglobin, low MCV (microcytic), low MCH (hypochromic), low serum iron, low ferritin (iron stores), high TIBC (transferrin). (3) Stages: Iron depletion (low ferritin, normal Hb) → iron-deficient erythropoiesis (low transferrin saturation) → iron deficiency anemia (low Hb). (4) Symptoms: Fatigue, pallor, pica (craving ice, dirt), koilonychia (spoon nails). (5) Treatment: Oral iron (ferrous sulfate); identify and treat underlying cause (GI bleed, etc.).

Significance: Iron deficiency anemia illustrates the importance of iron for erythropoiesis, the diagnostic approach to microcytic anemia, and the need to identify underlying causes.

image.alt: Microcytic hypochromic RBCs on smear. Iron studies: low ferritin, low iron, high TIBC. Pathophysiology: insufficient iron for heme synthesis. Causes: blood loss, inadequate intake.
image.caption: Iron Deficiency Anemia: Microcytic, hypochromic RBCs due to insufficient iron for hemoglobin synthesis. Ferritin (iron stores) is low; TIBC is elevated.

examples.explanation:
Sickle cell disease (SCD) is caused by a point mutation in β-globin (HbS): (1) Pathophysiology: Glu6Val mutation causes HbS polymerization under low O₂ conditions → RBC sickling (rigid, crescent-shaped). (2) Consequences: Hemolysis (shortened RBC lifespan, anemia), vaso-occlusion (pain crises, acute chest syndrome, stroke, avascular necrosis), functional asplenia (increased infection risk). (3) Diagnosis: Hb electrophoresis shows HbS (80-95%), HbF elevated; sickle cell test (solubility). (4) Treatment: Hydroxyurea (increases HbF, reduces crises), blood transfusions (stroke prevention), pain management, vaccination (encapsulated bacteria), stem cell transplantation (curative).

Significance: Sickle cell disease illustrates the molecular basis of a hematologic disorder (single amino acid substitution) and its systemic consequences (hemolysis, vaso-occlusion, organ damage).

image.alt: Sickled RBCs on blood smear. HbS polymerization under hypoxia. Clinical complications: pain crises, acute chest syndrome, stroke, asplenia. Hb electrophoresis shows HbS.
image.caption: Sickle Cell Disease: HbS mutation causes hemoglobin polymerization and RBC sickling under hypoxia, leading to hemolysis, vaso-occlusion, and multi-organ damage.

classifications.value: Best for understanding the classification, function, and clinical significance of white blood cells in infection, inflammation, and malignancy.
characteristics:
Granulocytes (Polymorphonuclear, PMNs): Neutrophils (50-70%)—multi-lobed nucleus, azurophilic granules (primary: myeloperoxidase, defensins) and specific granules (secondary: lactoferrin, lysozyme). Function: phagocytosis (bacteria, fungi); first responders to bacterial infection; form neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs). Elevated in bacterial infection, inflammation, stress (corticosteroids).
Eosinophils (1-4%)—bilobed nucleus, large eosinophilic granules (major basic protein). Function: anti-parasitic (helminths); allergic inflammation. Elevated in parasitic infection, allergy, asthma, eosinophilic disorders.
Basophils (<1%)—bilobed nucleus, basophilic granules (histamine, heparin). Function: allergic responses (IgE-mediated). Similar to mast cells.

Agranulocytes: Lymphocytes (20-40%)—round nucleus, scant cytoplasm. B cells: produce antibodies (plasma cells); humoral immunity. T cells: cellular immunity (CD4+ helper, CD8+ cytotoxic); regulatory T cells. NK cells: innate killing of virus-infected cells, tumor cells. Elevated in viral infections, chronic inflammation, lymphoproliferative disorders.
Monocytes (2-8%)—kidney-shaped nucleus; circulate 1-3 days then migrate to tissues as macrophages. Function: phagocytosis, antigen presentation, cytokine production. Elevated in chronic infections, inflammatory diseases, monocytic leukemias.

Production (Leukopoiesis): Myeloid lineage (neutrophils, eosinophils, basophils, monocytes) and lymphoid lineage (lymphocytes) derived from HSCs. Regulated by cytokines: G-CSF (neutrophils), GM-CSF (granulocytes, monocytes), IL-5 (eosinophils), interleukins (lymphocytes).
classifications.behavior:
Leukocytes mediate immune defense through distinct mechanisms. Neutrophils are the first responders to bacterial infection: they chemotax to infection sites, phagocytose bacteria, and release antimicrobial proteins. The neutrophil count increases (neutrophilia) in bacterial infection, inflammation, and stress. A "left shift" (increased band forms) indicates acute infection.

Lymphocytes mediate adaptive immunity: B cells produce antibodies against extracellular pathogens; T cells kill infected cells (CD8+) or orchestrate responses (CD4+). Lymphocytosis occurs in viral infections (EBV, CMV), chronic inflammation, and lymphoproliferative disorders. Lymphocytopenia (e.g., HIV) increases infection risk.

Monocytes migrate to tissues and differentiate into macrophages, which phagocytose pathogens, present antigens to T cells, and produce cytokines. Monocytosis occurs in chronic infections (tuberculosis), inflammatory diseases (rheumatoid arthritis), and monocytic leukemias.

Eosinophils combat helminth parasites and mediate allergic inflammation. Eosinophilia occurs in parasitic infections, asthma, allergic diseases, and eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA).

Clinical correlations: Leukocytosis (elevated WBC) occurs in infection, inflammation, stress, and hematologic malignancies (leukemia). Leukopenia (low WBC) occurs in bone marrow failure, chemotherapy, HIV, and certain infections. The differential (proportions of each WBC type) helps localize the underlying cause: neutrophilia → bacterial infection; lymphocytosis → viral infection; eosinophilia → parasite or allergy; monocytosis → chronic infection/inflammation; pancytopenia (low all cell lines) → bone marrow failure or infiltration. Leukemias are malignant proliferations of WBC precursors: acute myeloid leukemia (AML), acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL), chronic myeloid leukemia (CML), chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL).

examples.process: Acute Leukemia: Malignant Proliferation of WBC Precursors
examples.explanation:
Acute leukemia is characterized by accumulation of immature blast cells in bone marrow and peripheral blood: (1) Acute myeloid leukemia (AML): Myeloid blasts (Auer rods in some). Presents with fatigue (anemia), infections (neutropenia), bleeding (thrombocytopenia). Peripheral smear shows blasts; bone marrow >20% blasts. (2) Acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL): Lymphoid blasts; more common in children. (3) Pathophysiology: Genetic mutations (translocations, e.g., t(15;17) in acute promyelocytic leukemia) cause block in differentiation, increased proliferation. (4) Diagnosis: CBC (pancytopenia, blasts); flow cytometry (immunophenotyping); cytogenetics; molecular studies. (5) Treatment: Chemotherapy (induction, consolidation); targeted therapy (ATRA in APML); stem cell transplantation for high-risk disease.

Significance: Acute leukemia demonstrates the consequences of uncontrolled proliferation of hematopoietic precursors, leading to bone marrow failure (anemia, infection, bleeding) and the importance of WBC differential in diagnosis.

image.alt: Peripheral smear: blasts (large cells with high nuclear-cytoplasmic ratio). AML: Auer rods (myeloperoxidase granules). ALL: lymphoid blasts. Bone marrow aspirate showing hypercellularity with blasts.
image.caption: Acute Leukemia: Malignant proliferation of WBC precursors (blasts) causes bone marrow failure and requires urgent diagnosis and treatment.

classifications.value: Best for understanding platelet structure, function in hemostasis, and disorders of platelet number and function.
characteristics: Structure: Small (2-3 μm diameter), anucleate cell fragments derived from megakaryocytes. Contains granules: α-granules (adhesion proteins: vWF, fibrinogen; growth factors: PDGF, TGF-β) and dense granules (ADP, ATP, serotonin, Ca²⁺). Surface glycoproteins: GPIb (binds vWF), GPIIb/IIIa (integrin αIIbβ3, binds fibrinogen).

Hemostasis: Primary hemostasis (platelet plug formation): Adhesion (platelets bind exposed subendothelium via GPIb-vWF); Activation (shape change, granule release, thromboxane A₂ production); Aggregation (GPIIb/IIIa binds fibrinogen, linking platelets). Secondary hemostasis (coagulation cascade) stabilizes plug with fibrin. Platelet plug formation is rapid (seconds to minutes).

Production (Thrombopoiesis): Megakaryocytes in bone marrow undergo endomitosis (multiple nuclear divisions without cytokinesis) → large cells with polyploid nucleus; extend proplatelets into sinusoids; release platelets. Regulated by thrombopoietin (TPO), produced by liver (constitutively) and upregulated in thrombocytopenia. Lifespan: 7-10 days.
Clearance: Aged platelets removed by spleen (splenic sequestration).
classifications.behavior:
Platelets are essential for hemostasis and thrombosis. Under normal conditions, platelets circulate in an inactive state. Endothelial injury exposes subendothelial matrix (collagen, vWF) → platelet adhesion via GPIb-vWF → activation → shape change (spreading), granule release (ADP, thromboxane A₂) → recruitment of additional platelets → aggregation via GPIIb/IIIa-fibrinogen bridges → platelet plug (primary hemostasis). This plug is stabilized by fibrin (coagulation cascade).

Platelet disorders: Quantitative (abnormal number) or qualitative (abnormal function). Thrombocytopenia (platelets <150,000/μL) causes mucosal bleeding (petechiae, epistaxis, gingival bleeding), increased risk of hemorrhage. Causes: decreased production (bone marrow failure, chemotherapy, alcohol), increased destruction (immune thrombocytopenia—ITP, heparin-induced thrombocytopenia—HIT), sequestration (hypersplenism). Thrombocytosis (platelets >450,000/μL) increases thrombosis risk; reactive (infection, inflammation, iron deficiency) or primary (essential thrombocythemia, myeloproliferative neoplasm).

Qualitative platelet disorders: Bernard-Soulier syndrome (GPIb deficiency → impaired adhesion); Glanzmann thrombasthenia (GPIIb/IIIa deficiency → impaired aggregation); aspirin (irreversibly inhibits COX-1 → reduced thromboxane A₂ → impaired aggregation).

Clinical correlations: Immune thrombocytopenia (ITP)—autoantibodies against platelet surface glycoproteins → accelerated destruction by spleen; treatment: corticosteroids, IVIG, rituximab, thrombopoietin receptor agonists (eltrombopag). Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT)—antibodies against platelet factor 4 (PF4)-heparin complex → platelet activation, consumption, thrombosis (paradoxical thrombosis with thrombocytopenia). Aspirin—antiplatelet effect via COX-1 inhibition; used for secondary prevention of cardiovascular events. Antiplatelet agents: clopidogrel (P2Y12 inhibitor), abciximab (GPIIb/IIIa inhibitor).

examples.explanation:
ITP is an autoimmune disorder characterized by isolated thrombocytopenia: (1) Pathophysiology: Autoantibodies (IgG) against platelet surface glycoproteins (GPIIb/IIIa, GPIb/IX) → opsonization → accelerated clearance by splenic macrophages (Fc receptor-mediated). (2) Clinical: Isolated thrombocytopenia (normal RBCs, WBCs); bleeding manifestations: petechiae, purpura, epistaxis, menorrhagia; intracranial hemorrhage (rare, but risk with severe thrombocytopenia). (3) Diagnosis: CBC shows isolated low platelets; smear shows large platelets (megathrombocytes, compensatory production); rule out other causes (drugs, HIV, HCV, SLE). (4) Treatment: First-line: corticosteroids (prednisone) to reduce autoantibody production; IVIG for acute bleeding or prior to surgery (transient platelet increase). Second-line: rituximab (anti-CD20), thrombopoietin receptor agonists (eltrombopag, romiplostim) stimulate platelet production; splenectomy (removes site of destruction).

Significance: ITP illustrates the pathophysiology of autoimmune platelet destruction, the diagnostic approach to isolated thrombocytopenia, and the spectrum of treatments from immunosuppression to platelet stimulation.

image.alt: ITP: isolated thrombocytopenia, large platelets (megathrombocytes) on smear. Pathophysiology: autoantibodies opsonize platelets, splenic clearance. Treatment: corticosteroids, IVIG, TPO agonists, splenectomy.
image.caption: Immune Thrombocytopenia (ITP): Autoantibodies accelerate platelet destruction, causing isolated thrombocytopenia and bleeding risk. Treatment targets antibody production, clearance, or platelet production.

principles.principle: Hematopoiesis: Stem Cell Hierarchy and Lineage Commitment
principles.statement: All blood cells derive from a common hematopoietic stem cell (HSC) in bone marrow, which undergoes self-renewal and differentiation into myeloid and lymphoid lineages, driven by specific growth factors and transcription factors.
principles.application:
Functional inference: (1) HSCs are rare (1 in 10⁵-10⁶ bone marrow cells) but have extensive self-renewal capacity and can reconstitute the entire hematopoietic system (basis of bone marrow transplantation). (2) Lineage commitment is regulated by transcription factors: PU.1 (myeloid), GATA-1 (erythroid, megakaryocytic), Ikaros (lymphoid). (3) Growth factors: erythropoietin (RBCs), G-CSF (neutrophils), thrombopoietin (platelets), IL-7 (lymphocytes). (4) The hierarchy allows massive expansion: one HSC can generate 10¹² blood cells daily.

Clinical application: Bone marrow transplantation (HSC transplantation) uses the self-renewal and differentiation capacity of HSCs to reconstitute hematopoiesis after myeloablative therapy. Growth factors (EPO, G-CSF, TPO agonists) stimulate production of specific lineages. Myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS) and leukemias arise from genetic mutations in HSCs or progenitors, causing dysplasia and uncontrolled proliferation.
principles.example:
Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation (HSCT): (1) Indications: Leukemia, lymphoma, aplastic anemia, multiple myeloma, some immunodeficiencies. (2) Sources: Bone marrow (aspiration), peripheral blood (mobilized with G-CSF), cord blood. (3) Process: Conditioning (chemotherapy/radiation) eradicates malignant cells and immunosuppresses recipient; infusion of donor HSCs; engraftment (2-4 weeks). (4) Complications: Graft-versus-host disease (GVHD, donor T cells attack recipient tissues), infections (neutropenia), graft failure. (5) Allogeneic vs. autologous: Autologous (patient's own cells) avoids GVHD but may have residual malignant cells; allogeneic (donor) provides graft-versus-tumor effect.

Significance: HSCT demonstrates the clinical application of stem cell biology—the ability of HSCs to reconstitute the entire hematopoietic system after myeloablation.
principles.analogy: Hematopoiesis is like a tree: The hematopoietic stem cell (HSC) is the trunk; it branches into myeloid and lymphoid lineages (major branches); further branches produce specific cell types (leaves). Growth factors (like sunlight and water) direct which branches grow.

image.alt: Hematopoietic hierarchy: HSC → common myeloid progenitor (CMP) → erythrocyte, megakaryocyte, granulocyte-monocyte lineages; HSC → common lymphoid progenitor (CLP) → B cell, T cell, NK cell. Growth factors at each stage.
image.caption: Hematopoiesis: Hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) differentiate into myeloid and lymphoid lineages, producing all blood cells. Growth factors regulate lineage commitment and expansion.

principles.principle: Erythropoietin (EPO): Oxygen-Sensing Regulation of RBC Mass
principles.statement: Erythropoietin (EPO) is the primary regulator of erythropoiesis, produced by kidney in response to hypoxia; EPO stimulates RBC production to increase oxygen-carrying capacity—a classic feedback loop maintaining tissue oxygenation.
principles.application:
Functional inference: (1) Renal peritubular interstitial cells sense O₂ via HIF (hypoxia-inducible factor) pathway. Hypoxia (anemia, high altitude, lung disease) stabilizes HIF → EPO gene transcription → increased EPO → bone marrow erythropoiesis → increased RBC mass → increased O₂ delivery → feedback inhibition. (2) EPO receptor (EPOR) on erythroid progenitors (CFU-E, proerythroblasts) prevents apoptosis and promotes proliferation/differentiation.

Clinical application: Anemia of chronic kidney disease (CKD) results from insufficient EPO production (damaged renal interstitium); treated with recombinant EPO (epoetin alfa, darbepoetin). EPO abuse in endurance sports (blood doping) increases RBC mass and O₂-carrying capacity but increases thrombosis risk. Polycythemia vera (PV) is an EPO-independent myeloproliferative neoplasm (JAK2 mutation) causing uncontrolled RBC production. HIF stabilizers (roxadustat) are oral agents that stimulate endogenous EPO production for CKD anemia.
principles.example:
Anemia of Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD): (1) Pathophysiology: CKD damages renal peritubular interstitial cells → reduced EPO production → decreased RBC production → normocytic normochromic anemia. (2) Additional factors: Iron deficiency (blood loss, dialysis), inflammation (hepcidin), uremic inhibitors of erythropoiesis. (3) Treatment: Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESA): epoetin alfa or darbepoetin alfa to target Hb 10-11 g/dL (avoid >11 g/dL due to cardiovascular risk). Iron supplementation (IV or oral). (4) Monitoring: Hemoglobin, iron studies (ferritin, transferrin saturation). (5) Complications of ESA: Hypertension, thrombosis, pure red cell aplasia (rare, from anti-EPO antibodies).

Significance: Anemia of CKD illustrates the EPO feedback loop and its clinical manipulation with recombinant EPO to treat anemia.
principles.analogy: EPO regulation is like a thermostat: Hypoxia (low oxygen) is like a cold room; the kidney is the thermostat that senses the temperature; EPO is the signal to turn on the furnace (bone marrow); RBCs are the heat (oxygen); when oxygen returns, the thermostat turns off.

image.alt: EPO feedback loop: Hypoxia → kidney EPO production → bone marrow RBC production → increased O₂ delivery → feedback inhibition. CKD damages kidney → low EPO → anemia; treated with recombinant EPO.
image.caption: Erythropoietin (EPO) Feedback: Renal hypoxia triggers EPO production, stimulating RBC production to restore oxygen delivery. Anemia of CKD results from EPO deficiency; treated with recombinant EPO.

principles.principle: Hemostasis: Platelet Plug and Coagulation Cascade
principles.statement: Hemostasis involves a coordinated response: vasoconstriction, platelet adhesion/activation/aggregation (primary hemostasis), and activation of the coagulation cascade (secondary hemostasis) forming a fibrin mesh that stabilizes the platelet plug—balanced by fibrinolysis to prevent excessive thrombosis.
principles.application:
Functional inference: (1) Primary hemostasis (platelet plug): Endothelial injury → exposure of subendothelial vWF and collagen → platelet adhesion (GPIb-vWF), activation (shape change, granule release), aggregation (GPIIb/IIIa-fibrinogen). (2) Secondary hemostasis (coagulation): Tissue factor (TF) exposure activates extrinsic pathway → thrombin generation → fibrinogen → fibrin. (3) Fibrinolysis: Plasmin degrades fibrin, limiting clot size; tPA (tissue plasminogen activator) activates plasminogen.

Clinical application: Antiplatelet agents (aspirin, clopidogrel) inhibit primary hemostasis; used for arterial thrombosis prevention (MI, stroke). Anticoagulants (warfarin, heparin, DOACs) inhibit coagulation cascade; used for venous thromboembolism and atrial fibrillation. Bleeding disorders: von Willebrand disease (vWF deficiency, impaired adhesion); hemophilia A (factor VIII deficiency), hemophilia B (factor IX deficiency); platelet function disorders (Bernard-Soulier, Glanzmann). Thrombosis: arterial (platelet-rich) vs. venous (fibrin-rich) require different antithrombotic strategies.
principles.example: Hemophilia A: Coagulation Factor VIII Deficiency
principles.explanation:
Hemophilia A is an X-linked recessive disorder caused by factor VIII deficiency: (1) Pathophysiology: Factor VIII is a cofactor in intrinsic pathway (tenase complex: FVIIIa + FIXa activates FX). Deficiency → impaired thrombin generation → delayed fibrin formation. (2) Clinical: Deep tissue bleeding (hemarthrosis—joint bleeding, muscle hematomas), prolonged bleeding after trauma or surgery; severity correlates with FVIII level (severe <1%, moderate 1-5%, mild >5%). (3) Diagnosis: Prolonged aPTT (intrinsic pathway), normal PT; specific factor assays show low FVIII. (4) Treatment: Replacement therapy (recombinant FVIII or plasma-derived); prophylactic infusions prevent joint damage; emicizumab (bispecific antibody mimicking FVIIIa) for patients with inhibitors. (5) Complications: Inhibitors (neutralizing antibodies) develop in 20-30% of severe hemophilia A patients.

Significance: Hemophilia A illustrates the role of the coagulation cascade in secondary hemostasis, the clinical consequences of factor deficiency (deep tissue bleeding vs. platelet disorders which cause mucosal bleeding), and the use of factor replacement.
principles.analogy: Hemostasis is like repairing a leaky pipe: Primary hemostasis (platelet plug) is like quickly shoving a rag into the hole to stop the gush. Secondary hemostasis (coagulation) is like applying a sealant (fibrin) to permanently fix it. Fibrinolysis is like eventually removing the temporary patch once the repair is permanent.

image.alt: Hemostasis: primary (platelet adhesion, activation, aggregation) and secondary (coagulation cascade, fibrin formation). Antithrombotic targets: aspirin (platelet), heparin/warfarin (coagulation).
image.caption: Hemostasis: Primary (platelet plug) and secondary (coagulation cascade, fibrin) work together to stop bleeding. Antithrombotic drugs target different steps.

components.definition: Plasma (water, albumin, globulins, fibrinogen); Erythrocytes (hemoglobin, biconcave); Leukocytes (neutrophils, eosinophils, basophils, lymphocytes, monocytes); Platelets (megakaryocyte fragments, granules, glycoproteins).
components.equation: Hematocrit (Hct) = packed RBC volume / total blood volume × 100% (normal: male 40-54%, female 36-46%). Hemoglobin (Hb) = O₂-carrying capacity (normal: male 13.5-17.5 g/dL, female 12.0-15.5 g/dL). Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) = Hct / RBC count (normal 80-100 fL).
components.interpretation: Hematocrit and hemoglobin reflect RBC mass; low values indicate anemia. MCV classifies anemias: microcytic (MCV <80, iron deficiency, thalassemia), normocytic (80-100, anemia of chronic disease, blood loss), macrocytic (MCV >100, B12/folate deficiency, alcohol). RBC count, Hb, Hct, and indices (MCV, MCH, MCHC) guide anemia diagnosis. WBC count and differential guide infection/inflammation/leukemia evaluation. Platelet count guides bleeding/thrombosis risk assessment.

image.alt: Complete blood count (CBC) parameters: RBC count, Hb, Hct, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW; WBC count and differential; platelet count. Normal ranges. Anemia classification by MCV.
image.caption: Complete Blood Count (CBC): Quantifies formed elements and RBC indices. MCV classifies anemias as microcytic, normocytic, or macrocytic.

categories.type: Blood Groups: ABO and Rh Systems
categories.description: ABO and Rh blood groups are determined by surface antigens on RBCs; incompatibility causes transfusion reactions and hemolytic disease of the newborn (HDN).
categories.detailed_mechanism:
ABO system: A and B antigens are carbohydrates (H antigen modified by glycosyltransferases). Type A (A antigen), B (B antigen), AB (both), O (neither). Naturally occurring antibodies (IgM) against absent antigens develop in infancy (anti-A, anti-B). Transfusion of incompatible RBCs causes acute hemolytic reaction (complement-mediated).

Rh system: D antigen (Rh factor) is protein; Rh-negative individuals do not have D antigen. Anti-D antibodies (IgG) are not naturally occurring; develop after exposure (transfusion, pregnancy). Rh incompatibility in pregnancy: Rh-negative mother with Rh-positive fetus → sensitization at delivery → subsequent pregnancies: maternal anti-D crosses placenta → hemolytic disease of the newborn (HDN). Prevention: RhoGAM (anti-D immunoglobulin) given at 28 weeks and after delivery to prevent sensitization.

examples.process: Hemolytic Disease of the Newborn (HDN) due to Rh Incompatibility
examples.explanation:
Rh HDN occurs when Rh-negative mother is sensitized to Rh-positive fetal RBCs: (1) Sensitization: At delivery, fetal Rh-positive RBCs enter maternal circulation; mother produces anti-D IgG (months later). (2) Subsequent pregnancy: Maternal anti-D crosses placenta (IgG) and coats fetal Rh-positive RBCs → hemolysis (extravascular) → fetal anemia, hyperbilirubinemia (kernicterus risk), hydrops fetalis. (3) Prevention: Administer RhoGAM (anti-D immunoglobulin) at 28 weeks gestation and within 72 hours of delivery (or after any sensitizing event—amniocentesis, abortion). RhoGAM binds fetal RBCs, preventing maternal immune response. (4) Treatment of affected infant: Phototherapy (for hyperbilirubinemia), exchange transfusion (severe anemia, bilirubin).

Significance: Rh incompatibility illustrates the clinical importance of RBC antigens, the difference between IgM (natural) and IgG (immune) antibodies, and effective prevention through immunoglobulin administration.

image.alt: Rh incompatibility: Rh-negative mother, Rh-positive fetus; sensitization at delivery; subsequent pregnancy: maternal anti-D crosses placenta, causes fetal hemolysis. RhoGAM prevents sensitization.
image.caption: Hemolytic Disease of the Newborn (Rh HDN): Maternal anti-D antibodies cross placenta and cause fetal hemolysis. RhoGAM prevents sensitization.

categories.description: Hematologic malignancies arise from clonal proliferation of hematopoietic cells, classified by lineage (myeloid, lymphoid) and differentiation stage (acute, chronic).
categories.detailed_mechanism: Acute leukemias: Blast cells (immature precursors) accumulate; rapid onset. AML (myeloid blasts); ALL (lymphoid blasts). Chronic leukemias: More differentiated cells accumulate; slower onset. CML (myeloid, Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22)); CLL (lymphoid). Lymphomas: Solid tumors of lymphocytes; Hodgkin (Reed-Sternberg cells) and non-Hodgkin (B cell, T cell). Multiple myeloma: Malignant plasma cells; produce monoclonal immunoglobulin (M-protein); cause lytic bone lesions, renal failure, hypercalcemia, anemia.

examples.explanation:
CML is a myeloproliferative neoplasm characterized by the Philadelphia chromosome (Ph): (1) Genetics: Reciprocal translocation t(9;22)(q34;q11) fuses BCR (chromosome 22) with ABL1 (chromosome 9) → BCR-ABL fusion gene → constitutively active tyrosine kinase → uncontrolled proliferation, reduced apoptosis. (2) Clinical: Fatigue, weight loss, splenomegaly, leukocytosis (neutrophils, metamyelocytes, myelocytes, occasional blasts). (3) Phases: Chronic (controlled), accelerated, blast crisis (AML-like). (4) Diagnosis: Peripheral smear (leukocytosis, all stages of myeloid maturation); bone marrow; cytogenetics (Ph+); FISH or PCR (BCR-ABL). (5) Treatment: Tyrosine kinase inhibitors (imatinib, dasatinib, nilotinib) target BCR-ABL → excellent response; TKI resistance (mutations) managed with second/third-line TKIs; stem cell transplantation for refractory disease.

Significance: CML is a paradigm for targeted cancer therapy: identifying the molecular driver (BCR-ABL) and developing a specific inhibitor (imatinib) transformed prognosis from fatal to chronic manageable disease.

image.alt: Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22). BCR-ABL fusion gene constitutively activates tyrosine kinase. Peripheral smear: leukocytosis with myeloid precursors (myelocytes, metamyelocytes). Treatment: imatinib (tyrosine kinase inhibitor).
image.caption: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML): Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22) creates BCR-ABL fusion, driving uncontrolled proliferation. Tyrosine kinase inhibitors (imatinib) target BCR-ABL, achieving excellent responses.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Blood Composition Case Analysis

worked_examples.title: Blood Composition Case Analysis: Pancytopenia
worked_examples.problem: A 55-year-old woman presents with fatigue, easy bruising, and recurrent fevers. CBC: WBC 1,800/μL (normal 4,500-11,000), Hb 8.2 g/dL (normal 12-15.5), platelets 45,000/μL (normal 150,000-450,000). Peripheral smear shows normocytic normochromic RBCs, decreased granulocytes, and occasional blasts (5%). (1) What is the diagnosis based on these findings? (2) What is the significance of pancytopenia? (3) What is the significance of blasts on peripheral smear? (4) What diagnostic tests should be performed?

step_by_step.calculation: Pancytopenia: decreased WBCs (leukopenia), RBCs (anemia), platelets (thrombocytopenia). Normocytic normochromic anemia suggests bone marrow problem rather than nutritional deficiency. Presence of blasts (immature precursors) on peripheral smear indicates possible acute leukemia or myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS).

Pancytopenia indicates failure of trilineage hematopoiesis. Causes: (1) Bone marrow failure (aplastic anemia, MDS, leukemia); (2) Bone marrow infiltration (metastatic cancer, lymphoma); (3) Peripheral destruction (hypersplenism, immune destruction). The presence of blasts suggests bone marrow disorder (primary hematologic malignancy).

Peripheral blasts indicate that immature hematopoietic precursors are escaping the bone marrow. In acute leukemia, blasts accumulate and suppress normal hematopoiesis. The percentage of blasts in bone marrow is critical: acute myeloid leukemia (AML) requires ≥20% blasts (or ≥20% in peripheral blood). Myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS) have <20% blasts but dysplasia and cytopenias.

Bone marrow aspiration and biopsy: (1) Morphology: blast percentage, dysplasia, fibrosis. (2) Flow cytometry: immunophenotyping (myeloid vs. lymphoid markers; aberrant expression). (3) Cytogenetics: karyotype (e.g., t(15;17) in acute promyelocytic leukemia; complex karyotype in MDS). (4) Molecular studies: FLT3, NPM1, IDH1/2 mutations in AML. This patient likely has acute myeloid leukemia (AML) or high-risk MDS with excess blasts.

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Likely diagnosis: Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) or myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) with excess blasts. (2) Pancytopenia indicates bone marrow failure (normal hematopoiesis suppressed by malignant clone). (3) Blasts on peripheral smear indicate acute leukemia (≥20% blasts) or MDS. (4) Diagnostic tests: Bone marrow aspiration and biopsy with morphology, flow cytometry, cytogenetics, and molecular studies to confirm diagnosis and guide therapy.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Understanding normal blood composition (RBCs, WBCs, platelets) and the significance of pancytopenia guides evaluation of bone marrow disorders. The presence of blasts on smear indicates acute leukemia, requiring urgent bone marrow evaluation.

sections.introduction: Erythropoiesis is the process of red blood cell production, from hematopoietic stem cell to mature erythrocyte, regulated by erythropoietin (EPO) and requiring iron, vitamin B12, and folate.

process_flow.description: Hematopoietic stem cell (HSC) commits to erythroid lineage under influence of EPO and transcription factors (GATA-1).
process_flow.mechanism: HSC → common myeloid progenitor (CMP) → burst-forming unit-erythroid (BFU-E) → colony-forming unit-erythroid (CFU-E). EPO binds EPOR on CFU-E → prevents apoptosis, promotes proliferation.
process_flow.result: Erythroid progenitor committed to RBC production

image.alt: HSC → CMP → BFU-E → CFU-E. EPO promotes CFU-E survival and proliferation. GATA-1 drives erythroid differentiation.
image.caption: Step 1: Erythroid Commitment. HSCs commit to erythroid lineage under EPO and GATA-1, forming CFU-E (EPO-responsive progenitor).

process_flow.description: CFU-E differentiates through erythroblast stages (proerythroblast, basophilic, polychromatophilic, orthochromatic) with increasing hemoglobin synthesis.
process_flow.mechanism: Proerythroblast: large, basophilic cytoplasm, active hemoglobin synthesis. Basophilic erythroblast: intense basophilic cytoplasm (ribosomes). Polychromatophilic erythroblast: gray-blue cytoplasm (hemoglobin + RNA). Orthochromatic erythroblast: pink cytoplasm (hemoglobin), pyknotic nucleus. Requires iron (for heme), B12/folate (DNA synthesis).

image.alt: Erythroblast stages: proerythroblast, basophilic, polychromatophilic, orthochromatic. Increasing hemoglobin (pink), decreasing RNA (blue).
image.caption: Step 2: Erythroblast Differentiation. Cells progress through stages of hemoglobin synthesis, culminating in orthochromatic erythroblast with pyknotic nucleus.

process_flow.description: Orthochromatic erythroblast extrudes nucleus, forming reticulocyte (immature RBC with residual RNA).
process_flow.mechanism: Nuclear extrusion (enucleation) produces reticulocyte. Reticulocytes still contain ribosomes (RNA) visible as reticular network with supravital stain. Reticulocytes mature in bone marrow for 1-2 days, then circulate for 1 day before losing RNA to become mature RBC.

image.alt: Orthochromatic erythroblast extrudes nucleus; reticulocyte (with RNA network) enters circulation. Reticulocyte count reflects bone marrow response to anemia.
image.caption: Step 3: Enucleation. Orthochromatic erythroblast expels nucleus, forming reticulocyte. Reticulocytes mature to RBCs within 1-2 days. Reticulocyte count assesses erythropoietic activity.

process_flow.description: Reticulocyte loses RNA and organelles, becoming mature biconcave RBC with 120-day lifespan.
process_flow.mechanism: Reticulocyte loses RNA, mitochondria, ribosomes; acquires biconcave shape. Circulates for 120 days, then removed by splenic macrophages (extravascular hemolysis). Hemoglobin recycled: iron reused, heme converted to bilirubin, globin metabolized.
process_flow.result: Mature RBC with O₂ transport function

image.alt: Mature biconcave RBC. Senescent RBCs removed by spleen; hemoglobin catabolized to bilirubin (excreted) and iron (recycled).
image.caption: Step 4: Mature Erythrocyte. RBCs circulate for 120 days, then are cleared by the spleen. Hemoglobin breakdown products (bilirubin, iron) are recycled or excreted.

sections.definition: Molecular hematology encompasses the genetic and molecular mechanisms underlying blood cell production, function, and disease—enabling targeted therapies for hematologic malignancies and inherited blood disorders.

process_types.type: JAK-STAT Signaling in Hematopoiesis and Myeloproliferative Neoplasms
process_types.energy_profile: JAK-STAT signaling mediates cytokine receptor signaling (EPO, TPO, G-CSF, interleukins). Constitutive activation (JAK2 V617F mutation) drives myeloproliferative neoplasms (polycythemia vera, essential thrombocythemia, primary myelofibrosis).
process_types.direction: Cytokine (EPO, TPO) binds receptor → JAK2 activation (phosphorylation) → STAT5 phosphorylation → nuclear translocation → gene transcription (anti-apoptotic, proliferation). JAK2 V617F mutation (exon 14) causes constitutive JAK2 activation → cytokine-independent proliferation → polycythemia vera, ET, PMF.
process_types.function: Understand cytokine signaling; target JAK2 in myeloproliferative neoplasms with JAK inhibitors (ruxolitinib).
goals: Understand JAK-STAT signaling in normal hematopoiesis

primary_example.process: JAK2 V617F Mutation in Polycythemia Vera
primary_example.explanation:
Polycythemia vera (PV) is a myeloproliferative neoplasm characterized by JAK2 V617F mutation: (1) Pathophysiology: JAK2 V617F mutation (acquired, in >95% of PV) causes constitutive activation of JAK2 (independent of EPO/TPO). (2) Consequences: Erythroid progenitors proliferate without EPO → increased RBC mass; often also increased WBCs and platelets (panmyelosis). (3) Clinical: Hyperviscosity (headache, pruritus, plethora), thrombosis (stroke, MI, Budd-Chiari), bleeding (acquired von Willebrand). (4) Diagnosis: Elevated Hb/Hct, low EPO level, JAK2 V617F mutation. (5) Treatment: Phlebotomy (maintain Hct <45%), low-dose aspirin, cytoreduction (hydroxyurea, interferon, ruxolitinib). Ruxolitinib (JAK1/2 inhibitor) is used for JAK2-mutated PV or myelofibrosis.

Significance: JAK2 V617F exemplifies a molecular driver of hematologic malignancy, demonstrating how understanding signaling pathways leads to targeted therapy (JAK inhibitors).

image.alt: JAK-STAT signaling: EPO binds EPOR → JAK2 activation → STAT5 → gene transcription. JAK2 V617F mutation causes constitutive activation. Polycythemia vera: increased RBC mass, thrombosis. Treated with JAK inhibitor (ruxolitinib).
image.caption: JAK2 V617F Mutation in Polycythemia Vera: Constitutive JAK2 activation drives cytokine-independent erythroid proliferation. JAK inhibitors target this pathway.

image.alt: JAK-STAT signaling pathway: cytokine receptor, JAK2, STAT5, gene transcription. JAK2 V617F mutation constitutively activates pathway.
image.caption: JAK-STAT Signaling: Normal cytokine signaling; JAK2 V617F mutation causes constitutive activation, driving myeloproliferative neoplasms.

process_types.type: Gene Therapy for Inherited Blood Disorders
process_types.energy_profile: Gene therapy aims to correct genetic defects in hematopoietic stem cells, offering curative potential for sickle cell disease, β-thalassemia, and immunodeficiencies.
process_types.direction: Approaches: (1) Lentiviral vector transduction of autologous HSCs with functional gene (e.g., β-globin in β-thalassemia, anti-sickling hemoglobin in SCD). (2) CRISPR-Cas9 editing: disrupt BCL11A (a repressor of fetal hemoglobin) to increase HbF, ameliorating SCD and β-thalassemia. (3) Ex vivo HSC modification, followed by myeloablative conditioning and reinfusion.
process_types.function: Achieve durable correction of genetic defect, potentially curative without allogeneic donor.
goals: Understand principles of HSC gene therapy
Apply to sickle cell disease and thalassemia
process_types.energy_source: Gene therapy, molecular hematology, stem cell biology

primary_example.process: CRISPR-Cas9 Gene Editing for Sickle Cell Disease
primary_example.explanation:
Sickle cell disease (SCD) results from a single nucleotide mutation (β6 Glu→Val). Gene editing strategies: (1) Fetal hemoglobin (HbF) induction: BCL11A represses γ-globin (HbF) expression in adults. Disrupting the BCL11A erythroid enhancer (via CRISPR-Cas9) increases HbF, which inhibits HbS polymerization. (2) Clinical trial results (exa-cel): Autologous HSCs edited with CRISPR-Cas9 to disrupt BCL11A enhancer; after myeloablation, edited cells engraft; patients show high HbF levels (>20%) and elimination of vaso-occlusive crises. (3) Alternative approach: Correct the β-globin mutation directly (homology-directed repair) or insert an anti-sickling β-globin variant. (4) Advantages: Autologous (no GVHD), potentially curative. (5) Challenges: Long-term safety, cost, accessibility.

Significance: CRISPR-based gene therapy for SCD represents a paradigm shift—curative therapy for a common genetic disorder using precision genome editing in autologous HSCs.

image.alt: CRISPR-Cas9 editing of BCL11A erythroid enhancer in HSCs → increased HbF → inhibits HbS polymerization → prevents sickling. Clinical outcomes: high HbF, elimination of crises.
image.caption: CRISPR-Cas9 Gene Editing for Sickle Cell Disease: Disrupting BCL11A increases fetal hemoglobin (HbF), inhibiting HbS polymerization and preventing vaso-occlusive crises.

image.alt: Gene therapy process: harvest HSCs, edit (CRISPR or lentiviral vector), condition, reinfuse. Applications: SCD, β-thalassemia, SCID.
image.caption: Gene Therapy for Blood Disorders: Autologous HSCs are edited ex vivo and reinfused after myeloablation, offering curative potential for genetic blood disorders.

sections.relationship: Blood composition reflects the integrated function of hematopoietic stem cells, growth factors, and metabolic pathways. Erythrocytes transport oxygen; leukocytes mediate immunity; platelets maintain hemostasis. Disorders of blood composition—anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, hematologic malignancies—reflect disruptions in these processes. Molecular advances (JAK2 mutation in MPNs, CRISPR gene editing for SCD) enable targeted therapies and curative approaches, transforming the management of blood disorders.

summary:
Blood is a specialized fluid connective tissue composed of plasma (55%) and formed elements (45%): erythrocytes, leukocytes, and platelets.

Plasma (92% water, 7% proteins, 1% solutes): Albumin maintains oncotic pressure; globulins include antibodies (γ-globulins) and transport proteins; fibrinogen is essential for coagulation. Serum is plasma without clotting factors.

Erythrocytes (RBCs): Biconcave discs, no nucleus, packed with hemoglobin (Hb). Function: O₂ transport (cooperative binding, sigmoidal dissociation curve) and CO₂ transport (bicarbonate). Lifespan 120 days. Production (erythropoiesis) in bone marrow, regulated by erythropoietin (EPO, kidney). Requires iron, B12, folate. Anemia (low RBC/Hb) causes fatigue, pallor; classified by MCV: microcytic (iron deficiency, thalassemia), normocytic (anemia of chronic disease, blood loss), macrocytic (B12/folate deficiency). Polycythemia (high RBC) increases viscosity, thrombosis risk. Sickle cell disease: HbS mutation causes polymerization, hemolysis, vaso-occlusion.

Leukocytes (WBCs): Nucleated immune cells. Granulocytes: Neutrophils (50-70%, bacterial phagocytosis), eosinophils (1-4%, anti-parasitic, allergy), basophils (<1%, allergy). Agranulocytes: Lymphocytes (20-40%, B cells: antibody; T cells: cellular immunity; NK cells), monocytes (2-8%, differentiate to macrophages). Leukocytosis (high WBC) indicates infection, inflammation, or malignancy; leukopenia increases infection risk. Acute leukemias (AML, ALL) present with blasts, cytopenias.

Platelets: Anucleate fragments from megakaryocytes. Function: hemostasis—adhesion (GPIb-vWF), activation (shape change, granule release, thromboxane A₂), aggregation (GPIIb/IIIa-fibrinogen). Lifespan 7-10 days. Thrombocytopenia (low platelets) causes bleeding (petechiae, bruising); thrombocytosis increases thrombosis risk. Immune thrombocytopenia (ITP) is autoimmune platelet destruction. Hemophilia A (factor VIII deficiency) causes deep tissue bleeding (hemarthrosis).

Hematopoiesis: All blood cells derive from hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) in bone marrow. Myeloid lineage (RBCs, platelets, granulocytes, monocytes) and lymphoid lineage (B cells, T cells, NK cells). Growth factors: EPO (RBCs), G-CSF (neutrophils), TPO (platelets), interleukins (lymphocytes).

Blood groups: ABO system (A, B, AB, O) with naturally occurring IgM antibodies; Rh system (D antigen) with immune IgG antibodies. Rh incompatibility in pregnancy causes hemolytic disease of the newborn (HDN), prevented by RhoGAM.

Clinical correlations: Complete blood count (CBC) with differential evaluates RBC indices (MCV classifies anemia), WBC count and differential (infection, leukemia), platelet count (bleeding/thrombosis risk). Bone marrow aspiration/biopsy diagnoses leukemia, MDS, myeloma. Molecular advances: JAK2 V617F mutation in polycythemia vera; BCR-ABL in CML (targeted by imatinib); CRISPR gene editing for sickle cell disease.

Understanding blood composition—from plasma proteins to formed elements to molecular drivers of disease—is essential for diagnosing and managing hematologic and systemic disorders.
